title: IR-DA-03 — Offline Log Triage & Timeline Reconstruction (ORYXYBER) v1.1.1
description: You are given a compact case pack of exported Linux artifacts from a suspected compromise. There is no live target system. Your job is to extract key indicators and reconstruct what happened, using only the provided logs and evidence files. The pack contains realistic noise (legitimate logins, routine cron activity, and decoy entries) — only submit answers you can defend with evidence.
prerequisites: Familiarity with SSH authentication log patterns
Ability to read timestamps and correlate events across files
outcomes: Identify the primary brute-force source and the compromised account
Confirm the first successful password-based access from a public IP
Extract post-auth attacker actions (download, execution) from artifacts
Identify the persistence mechanism and validate it with evidence

levels.title: Briefing & Scope
levels.content:
### Briefing & scope

You are stepping in after the fact. The infrastructure team shut down the affected server and exported only a small bundle of artifacts.

Your job is to:
- confirm whether the system was accessed from the internet,
- identify the account that was used,
- determine what happened immediately after access, and
- spot any persistence that would survive a reboot.

**Important:** treat every answer as something you would put in an incident note - it must be backed by evidence from the case pack.

levels.title: Access the Sandbox
levels.local_content:
### Access

Use the credentials below to access the analyst workstation:

- **Username:** `user`
- **Password:** `Password123`

### Case pack location

The exported artifacts are here:

`/home/user/oryxyber/IR-DA-03/case_pack/`

Helpful starting commands:

```bash
cd /home/user/oryxyber/IR-DA-03/case_pack
ls -la
less auth.log
grep -n "Accepted" auth.log
```

When you are ready, enter the passkey **start** to begin.
levels.cloud_content:
### Access

Use the credentials below to access the analyst workstation:

- **Username:** `user`
- **Password:** `Password123`

### Case pack location

The exported artifacts are here:

`/home/user/oryxyber/IR-DA-03/case_pack/`

Helpful starting commands:

```bash
cd /home/user/oryxyber/IR-DA-03/case_pack
ls -la
less auth.log
grep -n "Accepted" auth.log
```

When you are ready, enter the passkey **start** to begin.

levels.title: Triage: Brute-force Source IP
levels.solution:
1. Open the case pack: `cd /home/user/oryxyber/IR-DA-03/case_pack`
2. Count SSH failures by source IP (works for both `Failed password` and `Invalid user` lines):
   ```bash
   grep -E "Failed password|Invalid user" auth.log \
     | sed -nE 's/.*from ([0-9]{1,3}(\.[0-9]{1,3}){3}).*/\1/p' \
     | sort | uniq -c | sort -nr | head
   ```
3. The top IP is your answer; submit it as `ORYX{IRDA03_SRCIP_<ip>}`.
levels.content:
### Task 1 — Brute-force source

From `auth.log`, determine the **single source IP** responsible for the majority of SSH authentication failures.

Submit the answer in this exact format:

`ORYX{IRDA03_SRCIP_<ip>}`

Example: `ORYX{IRDA03_SRCIP_1.2.3.4}`

hints.title: Where the IP sits
hints.content: In `auth.log`, SSH lines usually end with `from <ip> port <port> ssh2`.

hints.title: Keep it strict
hints.content: Include both failure patterns, but extract the IP with a regex (not a fixed awk field) to avoid counting the wrong column.

hints.title: If awk feels wrong
hints.content: Print a few raw lines and visually confirm which field is the IP.

levels.title: Triage: Compromised Username
levels.solution:
1. List successful password logins:
   ```bash
   grep "Accepted password" auth.log
   ```
2. Ignore internal management logins (10.x.x.x) and keep the internet-sourced one:
   ```bash
   grep "Accepted password" auth.log | grep -v " from 10\."
   ```
3. The username appears right after `for`.
levels.content:
### Task 2 — Compromised username

Identify the account that was successfully accessed **from the public brute-force source**.

Submit only the username (no flag wrapper).

hints.title: Accepted lines matter
hints.content: Look for `Accepted password` rather than `Accepted publickey`.

hints.title: Public vs internal
hints.content: There is at least one internal login in the log; your answer must match the internet-sourced access.

levels.title: Corroboration: First Password Login Timestamp
levels.solution:
1. Identify the first successful password login from a public IP:
   ```bash
   grep "Accepted password" auth.log | grep -v " from 10\." | head -n 1
   ```
2. Take the timestamp (month/day/time) from that line and format it exactly as requested.
levels.content:
### Task 3 — First confirmed compromise time

Find the **first successful password-based SSH login** coming from a **public IP**.

Submit in this exact format:

`ORYX{IRDA03_FIRSTPASS_FEB19_021407}`

Tip: the required value is the timestamp from the matching `Accepted password` line.

hints.title: Format matters
hints.content: Use `FEB19_021407` style exactly (month in caps, no spaces).

levels.title: Corroboration: Payload Download URL
levels.solution:
1. Search for payload retrieval commands (they may appear in `bash_history` and also in `sudo` lines inside `auth.log`):

```bash
grep -RIn "curl -fsSL\|wget " .
```

2. Extract the host/IP and the downloaded file name from the URL.
3. Submit: `ORYX{IRDA03_URL_<ip>_<file>}`.
levels.content:
### Task 4 — Payload retrieval

Determine the **download URL** used to retrieve the initial payload from the case pack.

To keep answers short, submit **only the host/IP and the file name** in this exact format:

`ORYX{IRDA03_URL_<ip>_<file>}`

Example: `ORYX{IRDA03_URL_198.51.100.23_patch.sh}`

hints.title: Don’t overthink it
hints.content: The URL appears verbatim in at least one artifact.

hints.title: Anchor on /tmp/.upd
hints.content: Find where `/tmp/.upd` was created or executed, then look left for the URL.

levels.title: Persistence: Cron Registration Path
levels.solution:
1. Review scheduled execution evidence:
   ```bash
   grep -n "update.sh" cron.log
   ```
2. Identify how it was registered by checking `auth.log` for the file that was written under `/etc/cron.d/`.
3. Confirm the matching config file exists in `evidence/` (it may be hidden):
   ```bash
   ls -la evidence
   ```
4. Submit the exact path as `ORYX{IRDA03_PERSIST_<path>}`.
levels.content:
### Task 5 — Persistence mechanism

Identify the persistence mechanism that ensures repeated execution.

You must submit the **exact file path** of the persistence configuration in this format:

`ORYX{IRDA03_PERSIST_<path>}`

Example: `ORYX{IRDA03_PERSIST_/etc/cron.d/example}`

hints.title: Cron leaves fingerprints
hints.content: You should see both a config file and execution evidence.

hints.title: Exact path required
hints.content: Use the full `/etc/...` path, including the leading dot in the filename.

levels.title: Validation: Payload SHA256
levels.solution:
1. Identify the recovered payload file under `evidence/`.
2. Compute the hash:

```bash
sha256sum evidence/payload_.upd
```

3. Take the **first 12** hex characters from the SHA256 output.
4. Submit it as `ORYX{IRDA03_SHA256_<first12>}` (lowercase).
levels.content:
### Task 6 — Validate the recovered payload

The recovered payload is included in the case pack under `evidence/`.

Compute the **SHA256** of the file and submit **only the first 12 hex characters** in this format:

`ORYX{IRDA03_SHA256_<first12>}`

Example: `ORYX{IRDA03_SHA256_deadbeefcafe}`

hints.title: Use sha256sum
hints.content: The file is small; hashing should be instant.

hints.title: Lowercase only
hints.content: If your tool prints lowercase already, keep it as-is.

levels.title: Assessment
levels.instructions: Answer the following questions based on your investigation.

questions.text: Which artifact is the most reliable starting point to confirm the first successful SSH login in this case?
Which evidence best supports that persistence was established?

choices.text: A single 'Failed password' entry in auth.log
A routine logrotate message in cron.log
A normal package maintenance entry in syslog

questions.text: Which command was used to download the second-stage script in the post-auth activity?
Which file in the evidence bundle most directly indicates SSH key-based persistence for root?
